core_intuitions: A GPU is a THROUGHPUT machine: thousands of arithmetic lanes fed by a comparatively thin memory pipe, hiding latency by instantly switching to another ready warp instead of caching/predicting like a CPU.

The execution model is a strict nesting mapped onto hardware: grid > block(CTA) > warp(32 lockstep SIMT lanes) > thread maps to GPU > SM > scheduler > lane; the 32-lane warp is the REAL unit of execution, so divergence and uncoalesced access are the silent throughput killers.

There are TWO arithmetic engines: CUDA cores do one scalar FMA per cycle, Tensor Cores consume whole tiles and emit a whole tile matmul-accumulate per instruction, which is why matmul throughput is ~16x scalar throughput -- but only for reduced-precision matmuls, everything else stays on CUDA cores and is memory-bound.
The memory hierarchy (registers > SMEM/L1 > L2 > HBM) drops ~10x in speed and rises ~10x in size per tier; keeping reused data in the fast tiers is the whole game (the FlashAttention insight).

Arithmetic intensity I = FLOPs/HBM-bytes versus the ridge point pi/beta decides compute- vs memory-bound: big matmul sits far right (compute-bound), single-token decode sits at I~1 (memory-bound), and batching slides decode rightward across the ridge.
The ridge point keeps CLIMBING every GPU generation because compute grows faster than bandwidth, so more and more workloads fall on the memory-bound side over time.

existing.name: memory-hierarchy
existing.issue: The core idea (three on-chip tiers all at tens of TB/s, then a ~10x bandwidth cliff to HBM while capacity balloons) reads clearly in both themes and the bar-length=bandwidth encoding is strong. Two concrete defects: (1) the left rotated label 'off-chip DRAM' sits at x=8 and is clipped by the left viewBox edge in both themes, and it is ALSO duplicated as an inline label inside the HBM bar -- redundant and messy. (2) The math glyphs proportional-to, approximately-equal and right-arrow render as empty tofu boxes in the raster/fallback font ('bar length [] speed', 'each tier [] 10x larger going down []', 'on-chip [] off-chip'), so the annotations partly break.
existing.improvement: Nudge the whole drawing right (add ~16px left padding / move the rotated axis label to x>=18 so it clears the edge) and delete the duplicate inline 'off-chip DRAM' inside the HBM bar, keeping only one scope label. Replace the tofu-prone glyphs with font-robust equivalents: use the word 'bandwidth (bar length = speed)' or an SVG line+arrowhead instead of the proportional-to sign, write 'each tier ~10x larger going down' with a drawn arrow marker rather than the unicode arrow, and 'on-chip -> off-chip: ~10x' as a drawn arrow. This keeps the (already good) intuition intact while removing the clipping and the broken glyphs.

new_figures.name: roofline-intensity-decode-vs-matmul
new_figures.concept: The roofline: attainable performance vs arithmetic intensity, with the ridge point, and the two canonical workloads (compute-bound matmul, memory-bound decode) plus the batching sweep that carries decode across the ridge.
new_figures.rationale: This is explicitly THE master concept of the chapter ('the idea that unifies this entire chapter and most of Part IV') and the chapter currently has zero visual for it. The roofline is the single mental model that turns every downstream claim ('decode is memory-bound', 'batch to light up the Tensor Cores') into geometry a reader can point at. Making the log-log slope, the flat ceiling, the ridge, and the enormous gap between I~1 (decode) and I~2700 (matmul) visible is what makes the compute- vs memory-bound dichotomy click.
new_figures.placement: ## Arithmetic Intensity: The Master Concept (anchor after the roofline dichotomy paragraph that ends '...You are FLOP-limited.', before the 'why decode is memory-bound and matmul is not' worked example)
new_figures.brief: A log-log roofline chart. X axis: arithmetic intensity I in FLOP/byte, spanning a wide range from ~1 up to a few thousand (label a few gridlines: 1, 10, 100, 1000). Y axis: attainable performance in FLOP/s (or TFLOP/s), also log. Draw the roofline as two joined line segments: a rising diagonal on the left (the memory-bandwidth roof, slope = peak bandwidth beta; annotate it 'memory-bound: P = I x beta, limited by HBM bandwidth') that bends at the RIDGE POINT into a horizontal ceiling on the right (the compute roof at peak FLOP/s pi; annotate 'compute-bound: P = pi, limited by Tensor Cores'). Mark the ridge point clearly with a dashed vertical drop line and label it 'ridge point = pi / beta ~ hundreds of FLOP/byte' (use a small illustrative integer like ~150-300, do NOT invent a precise benchmark number -- 'a few hundred' phrasing is fine). Place two workload markers ON or under the roof: (1) a dot far to the RIGHT under the flat ceiling labeled 'large matmul: I ~ thousands -> COMPUTE-bound, near peak', sitting right at the ceiling; (2) a dot far to the LEFT low on the diagonal labeled 'single-token decode: I ~ 1 -> MEMORY-bound, Tensor Cores ~idle', sitting on the rising diagonal near the far left, with a small caption 'Tensor Cores ~99% idle here'. Shade or tint the region left of the ridge as 'memory-bound' and right of the ridge as 'compute-bound' (use theme-safe muted fills, no hardcoded brand colors). ANIMATION (enhancement only; the single static frame must be fully legible without it): a highlighted decode marker starts at I~1 and steps rightward along the diagonal as a small 'batch = 1 -> 8 -> 32 -> 128 -> 256' counter increments, climbing the diagonal until it reaches the ridge and then rides along the flat ceiling -- visually showing that batching raises intensity (weight bytes constant, FLOPs scale with batch) until decode finally becomes compute-bound. Add a short caption tying it together: 'batching slides the same weight-load rightward until the Tensor Cores light up'. Must render correctly and read the same story in light and dark themes; keep all labels inside the viewBox, no overlapping text.

new_figures.name: execution-model-warp-simt
new_figures.concept: The software execution hierarchy (grid > block/CTA > warp of 32 > thread) mapped onto hardware (GPU > SM > warp scheduler > lane), with a zoom into one warp showing 32 lanes executing the SAME instruction in lockstep (SIMT), and the coalesced-vs-scattered memory access contrast.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls understanding this hierarchy 'non-negotiable' and names warp divergence and uncoalesced access as 'the two most common silent throughput killers.' The existing ASCII block is functional but flat; a real infographic that shows the nesting AND the mapping to hardware AND why the 32-lane warp is the true unit of execution builds the structural intuition every later performance claim leans on.
new_figures.placement: ## The Execution Model: Threads, Warps, Blocks, and SMs (anchor after the '### The grid and the launch' paragraph and its ASCII, i.e. just before the saxpy python block)
new_figures.brief: A two-part static infographic. TOP: a left-to-right (or nested-boxes) mapping showing the software hierarchy stacked against the hardware it runs on. Software column: GRID (many blocks) contains BLOCK / CTA contains WARP (32 threads) contains THREAD. Hardware column, aligned row-for-row: GPU (many SMs) -> SM (one block lives here its whole life, owns the register file + shared memory) -> WARP SCHEDULER (picks a ready warp each cycle) -> LANE (one thread). Draw arrows or aligned brackets making the mapping explicit (Grid->GPU, Block->SM, Warp->scheduler, Thread->lane). Annotate the block box with 'threads in a block share fast SMEM + can __syncthreads()' and note 'different blocks cannot cheaply share/sync -> run independently -> scales'. BOTTOM (the payoff zoom): blow up ONE warp as a row of 32 small lane cells. Show all 32 lanes pointing at ONE shared instruction (a single 'fetch/decode + program counter' box feeding all lanes) with the caption 'SIMT: one instruction, 32 lanes, lockstep -- the warp is the real unit of execution.' Add two compact side-by-side mini-panels illustrating the two silent killers: (a) DIVERGENCE -- an if-split where ~half the lanes take path A and half path B, drawn as the warp running path A with the other lanes greyed/masked, THEN path B with the first half masked, labeled 'both sides run serially -> up to 2x slower'; (b) COALESCING -- two little rows of 32 lanes hitting memory: one where lane i reads address base+i (contiguous) collapsing into '1-2 transactions (coalesced, near peak BW)', and one where the 32 lanes read scattered addresses fanning out into 'up to 32 transactions (32x penalty)'. Use small illustrative integers only (32 lanes, 1-2 vs 32 transactions). Keep it uncluttered with clear top-to-bottom reading order; must be legible and theme-safe in both light and dark, all content inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: cuda-core-vs-tensor-core
new_figures.concept: Why Tensor Cores are ~16x CUDA cores: a scalar CUDA core does one FMA (a*b+d) per cycle, while a Tensor Core consumes whole small tiles A, B, C and emits a whole tile D = A@B + C per instruction -- but only for reduced-precision matmul.
new_figures.rationale: Conflating the two engines is called 'the most common beginner error in performance reasoning,' and Tensor Core utilization is what 'every modern training and inference workload lives or dies on.' A single side-by-side that contrasts one scalar FMA against one tile-MAC instruction makes the order-of-magnitude gap, and its narrow applicability (matmul only, reduced precision, tile-shaped data), visually obvious -- underpinning the whole 'keep the Tensor Cores fed' and kernel-fusion story.
new_figures.placement: ## CUDA Cores vs Tensor Cores (anchor after the '### Tensor Cores: matrix-multiply-accumulate engines' paragraph, before the note aside about throughput numbers)
new_figures.brief: A side-by-side contrast of the two arithmetic engines, two panels sharing a common 'per instruction / per cycle' framing so the throughput gap is the visual takeaway. LEFT panel 'CUDA core (scalar ALU)': show a single small unit computing c = a*b + d, i.e. one FMA on individual scalars, labeled 'one fused multiply-add per cycle, FP32/INT'. To emphasize the cost of doing a tile this way, add a faint note: 'a 16x16x16 tile the scalar way = issue 16^3 = 4096 scalar FMAs.' RIGHT panel 'Tensor Core (matrix engine)': show three small input tiles A, B, C (draw them as little grids, e.g. 16x16 boxes, or a compact 4x4 stand-in with a '16x16' label) flowing into a systolic-array-like block that emits one output tile D, with the equation D = A @ B + C, labeled 'one instruction consumes whole tiles -> a whole tile of MACs.' Between/under the panels place the headline comparison as a bar or callout: the Tensor Core matmul throughput is roughly an ORDER OF MAGNITUDE (~16x) the CUDA-core FP32 throughput on the SAME hardware -- draw two proportional bars (short vs ~16x long) to make it visceral; label them 'FP32 CUDA cores' (short) and 'BF16/FP16 Tensor Cores' (~16x). Use only illustrative ratios (1x vs ~16x), do NOT print invented absolute TFLOP/s numbers. Add a boxed 'THE CATCH' caption listing the constraints: 'Tensor Cores accelerate only matmul, only in reduced precision (BF16/FP16/FP8/FP4, FP32 accumulate), on tile-shaped data with dims multiple of 8/16. Pointwise ops (add, GELU, norm) get ZERO benefit -> CUDA cores -> memory-bound.' Keep two clean columns with clear labels; theme-safe colors, everything inside the viewBox, legible in light and dark.

summary: The book's most important systems chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: it has exactly one figure (memory-hierarchy) for a chapter whose stated master concept (the roofline) and whose 'non-negotiable' execution model have no visual at all. Audit: memory-hierarchy is fundamentally good (bar-length=bandwidth, the ~10x cliff to HBM reads well in both themes) but needs a refine -- the rotated left axis label is clipped at the viewBox edge and duplicated inside the HBM bar, and several math glyphs (proportional-to, approx, arrows) render as tofu boxes; fix by padding the left margin, deleting the duplicate label, and swapping the tofu glyphs for drawn arrows / ASCII. Three new foundational figures are proposed, all tied directly to core intuitions the text names as load-bearing: (1) a roofline chart with ridge point plus the compute-bound matmul and memory-bound decode markers and an animated batching sweep -- THE unifying concept of the chapter and all of Part IV (P1); (2) a static execution-model infographic mapping grid>block>warp>thread onto GPU>SM>scheduler>lane, with a warp zoom showing 32-lane SIMT lockstep plus the divergence and coalescing 'silent killers' (P1); (3) a static CUDA-core-vs-Tensor-core contrast (one scalar FMA vs one whole-tile matmul-accumulate) making the ~16x gap and its narrow matmul-only applicability visceral (P2). This is disciplined: three figures for the three most-emphasized mechanisms, no decoration.
